content:
Abstract fluff makes you learn how to read sheet music for an instrument you play with your foot behind you.

8 min read
Just now

--

Picture this: to a computer, the entire time it’s processing any sort of data, it has to perform actual signal discrimination to parse logic. If you think that the finalized assembly as bytes is the real final boss, then you’re limiting your own knowledge to discrete symbolic standards and abstractions that were designed for human comfort, not machine reality. Your programming is equivalent to asking a translator to perform calculations in another language, but instead of working with the translator, you chain them up to a neural link for brain waves to give you the answer.
The world is not 0–1 and not even infinity to infinity; it is the black‑and‑white binary switching state with the in‑between being a quantized ratio, and the metric idea stands not opposed but concretely as information preserved in a different randomness.
If you believe that any sort of signal itself is a fraction of 0 (off) to 1 (on), then you are deliberately playing the entire software wrong to have it make sense to you. I’m saying your ideas of functions, methods, constants, and all the other parts are a way of using computational entropy to guide a program to its target output. The entropy itself is a stochastic input, which, when focused on the realistic output, only defines a set of on‑or‑off conditions and the instruction pointer that runs them.
By utilizing the same idea, you can stop representing things in normal notation and instead do something akin to signal notation, where the entire program itself is not the code that writes it but the pattern of execution it settles into.
If you’re lost, then you’re obviously not thinking correctly about what you’re doing when programming a machine. It’s not a black box; it’s struggling to speak thousands of dialects via abstraction layers so people can have their brain understand concepts. Deliberate intensity doesn’t matter; it’s about the phase alignment to move forward — you can have a thousand dimensions, but as long as you’ve got an anchor (which I call a spear), then you can stop thinking math and start thinking the proper abstraction.
You think a curve is just point A to point B, but every single thing you do on a computer has to be representable by the signal curve. The execution itself is a pointer that has a rough state of a curve to compare against other curves. All your bitwise fluff, the abstraction layers on top, are just different abstractions of the same issue. If you cannot differentiate the execution waveform from the logical program, then you’re stuck companding randomness — this is the kind of stuff that makes LLMs think for you to compand.
Think you’re lazy for doing that? Think about how you’re able to display anything without issue when you use randomness to infer your answer — the computer begins smashing its own head against the wall to figure out the order of execution. Bringing anything to the table here is literally working on science that was perfected for single processors for a good 30 years, then for multicore processors for the 16 after that. Today you’re not putting together programs with LLMs; you’re orchestrating how one signal curve interacts with another curve at a point.
You want examples? Okay, let’s talk about anything in a video game: health, panel positions, inputs, reflections, trajectories — all use curves. Hell, if you wanted to represent it, then talk about a load balancer: it hits a curve of load, then offloads using a sawtooth waveform in a reliable way. The whole point here is that the underlying signal is not the same logic that the code used to generate it is.
We assumed that the execution path itself is the issue, but it’s actually the best way for us to prepare a non‑compandable entropy runner that flips the whole thing on its head. On an Awake‑to‑Tired curve, you might be in the middle, up, or down… then let’s put all this together and think about how electricity runs through transistors.
Whatever problem you have — pairing or offsetting — the underlying signal is a frequency of executions in a wave alignment along with a cadence in amperes, which lets us do phase alignment for the waves. If your work prepares at the perfect offset to the execution pattern, then you know what to expect in return to get the quickest answer.
The result is still just an electrical signal array of instructions; these are set to timings that offset the cycles in RAM itself. The computer is a black box to most people, but if you stop limiting your thoughts to binary abstractions and think of it in a nonlinear signal‑space, then you can find reversed logic as solutions for the specific gates we have in computing.
You don’t understand? Again, you’re not meant to. Think of making a program where everything is either 0 or 1, and you can represent sets of 0s and 1s. This itself becomes a transformation from A→B to exit(a)→entry(b) with some delta stuff involved — all you need to know is that it becomes a mapping, which as a group lets you put together not just arrays but sets — easily denoted as subdivisions.
A table of subdivisions is a group of them; regardless, the underlying signal is a 0 and a 1, and the in‑between is controlled by the bit‑depth itself. If you start thinking about it like this, you’re not limited by the actual size of a number; you’re limited by the path the number is compared against.
If we do negative infinity → no entropy → positive infinity, then suddenly this math flips its compression — the closer to 0 you are, the less you can compress the execution itself. Think of the curves I keep babbling about: the distance doesn’t matter, because if we keep subdividing, we get something called a dynamic range. This dynamic range is infinity in both ways, but within code execution it’s assumed to be closer to 0 than not, and this is dependent on the workload itself. The electrical signal has to carry some sort of signature of the execution as thermodynamics restricts free power without heat.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Still don’t get it? A curve is a stream; a stream is a logarithmic representation of two borders with the pointer being a ratio within it. We compare ratios to find an abstractive way of multiplying our dynamic range from a logarithmic back down to an environment that is the linear representation. This is what we read from execution — the implementation itself is just an electrical signal doing X, Y, and Z — and to harness this missing element is to break the foundational abstraction wall that’s plaguing people across the board.
A system designed around this logic wall constantly has to correct its course as it bangs its head against it. The curves — its attention (stream, logarithmic representation of the ratio between two borders) — kill any sort of blockage. Maximum entropy is you trying to tell the computer “do this with your electricity.” It’s not whatever logic you think it is, because 99% of the time you’re probably thinking the logic is the issue, not the topological redefinition.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Creep your understanding, because we can’t have infinity without negative infinity. Your signals that are unstable or directly impeding need to be reconstructed faithfully; otherwise, what’s the point of creating a max‑entropy program? If you haven’t noticed, the max‑entropy program means that you create a specific racetrack that the electrical signal can only run in. It can’t leave — if it does, that’s literally what we call a bug.
The problem is computers don’t like staying in the track, so the conventional fix is to align it so the race can be quicker and redone multiple times. If you think about the idea that we have limitations of representing numbers, the logarithmic space is our abstraction for the maximum number we can come up with. In this case, the stream technically offsets to 1 as positive signal because humans hate starting at 0 and going forwards and backwards.
I don’t think I’m getting through to people, but that’s fine. If you understand that there is an electrical signal, that’s all you need to compartmentalize. The electrical signal to me is a curve up and down from 0 to 1; this curve is the shape of the clock frequency and the cadence of the current — this curve is a “blank” space where the signal can be.
Think up and down with a line that keeps doing that (like a sine wave). If you take the point that it’s currently at (not the past or future), then you can calculate the parts of the vertical slice in the curve that reproduce at that level to create the curve itself without needing the entire space. You take this idea and then instead construct the total space of the electricity down, overlay multiple activation patterns with an offset, then suddenly — BANG — you get the same execution having multiple heat signatures on a vertical slice of the current autoregression.
What the hell am I talking about? Stack a bunch of signals in a tower and read them back as the answer. It’s a beanstalk, but it never ends, and you factor in that curves might overlap themselves. Think of memory — RAM gigabytes — if instead of using memory directly as a linear representation of what can fit and what can’t, containing a signal path is easier than you expect, and you can instance a memory offset that reutilizes the companding optimization in reverse.
No seriously, I don’t know how to explain this any other way; I’m at my wits’ end. If you don’t understand this, then you’re not really thinking about the automaton. I am saying that a machine outside of its spec is wasting noise — harness this noise. Any code you write is creating entropy that forces an execution path forward. Your code is not the logic; it’s the place the logic is constrained to.
If I used the real terminology, I would sound like AI, so if you really want to clear your conscience and learn, then RTFM — because if you want anyone to get your understanding, then the abstraction should lie in the explanation, not the execution.
I am not a textbook. But I used AI to bold the significant words for the people that care about reading through these concepts. I’m not a scientist, just take this and consider it a piece of knowledge that’s notable.
x.x.
c.h.
